00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Śmiech)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Wyśmialiście się właściwie.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319
Naprawdę mi pomogło z tego wyjść… gra *Mario Kart*.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
Ale poprzednia historia:

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
Na początku pandemii

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
wielu z nas walczyło z lękiem, smutkiem lub odizolowaniem.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
Ale kiedy pandemiczna okazała się bez końca,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
nasz gorący ból się na dłużej przedłużył do zamyślonego spowolnienia.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
Wszyscy żyliśmy w trybie *„Dnia Mikołaja”*.

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
Cały świat stał w miejscu.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
Więc napisałem artykuł o spowolnieniach.

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
Nazwałem go *„małą siostrą zdrowia psychicznego, którą ignorują”*

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
i zaproponowałem, że może to uczucie naszego czasu.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
I szybko zaczęło się roznosić.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
Znosiłem go wszędzie w mediach,

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
wypowiadali na niego gwiazdy i nawet królowie.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
Nigdy nie widziałem ludzi tak wciągniących

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
w rozmowy o braku w nich emocji.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Śmiech)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
I…

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
Myślę…

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
Że nazwa spowolnienie pomogła ludziom zrozumieć dziwne przeżycia.

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
Dlaczego nawet po szczepieniach

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042
ludzie nie umieli wyobrazić sobie zakończenia roku.

61
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462
Dlaczego kiedy włączałem *National Treasure* TV,

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
mogłem wstać, wiedząc wszystkie teksty na pamięć.

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
Dlaczego walczyłem się, żeby iść spać tak późno?

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
Cofnąłem się do *„rewanżowego spowolnienia nocnego”*.

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475
*(revenge bedtime procrastination)*

66
00:03:07,516 --> 00:03:08,768
(Śmiech)

67
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
Szukaliśmy szczęścia w pustych dniach i celów w bezcelowej pandemii.

68
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
Ale spowolnienie nie tylko to jest pandemii.

69
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,704
To część ludzkiego doświadczenia.

70
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166
Nauka z ostatnich dziesięcioleci pokazuje, że spowolnienie

71
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252
zamyśla nas, drze po motywacji.

72
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,671
Jest też czynnikiem ryzyka dla depresji

73
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
bo spowalnienie kryje się niżej.

74
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
Nie zauważasz, jak twoja energia maleje,

75
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
jak to proste radości nie dają już radości.

76
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
Jesteś obojętny na swoją obojętność,

77
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
więc nie szukasz pomocy

78
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
i może nawet nic nie uczynisz dla siebie.

79
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
*„Tak sobie… tak sobie…”*

80
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
Spowolnienie nie tylko trudno dostrzec.

81
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
W wielu kulturach trudno z nim o nie przeszkadzać.

82
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
Kiedy ludzie pyta ich: „Jak się masz?”

83
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
mają odpowiedzieć: *„Świetnie!”*

84
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
lub: *„Kończę swój najlepszy dzień.”*

85
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
To nazywamy *„toksycznym pozytywizmem”*.

86
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Śmiech)

87
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
To presja, żeby być optymistą i „bliźni” ni wbrew sobie.

88
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
Kiedy mówisz: *„No… raczej tak sobie”,*

89
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
ludzie podsuwają ci, żeby „patrzeć pozytywnie”

90
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475
lub „wyliczać dary życia” —

91
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935
co nie tylko drażni.

92
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
Może być też szkodliwe.

93
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
Potrzebuję dwóch wolontariuszy.

94
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276
Wzywam na scenę — nie wkurwiajcie mnie.

95
00:04:40,276 --> 00:04:41,902
Dobra, tam ten przy drzwiach.

96
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
Podczasze do mikrofonu…

97
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
I jeszcze jeden, ów na końcu — weź ten drugi mikrofon.

98
00:04:46,991 --> 00:04:49,035
Brawa dla naszych ofiar.

99
00:04:49,076 --> 00:04:50,870
(Brawa)

100
00:04:52,872 --> 00:04:54,373
Cześć. Jak masz na imię?

title: The link between menopause and gender inequity at work
summary:
1
00:00:01,831 --> 00:00:06,961
W 500 największych amerykańskich firmach, zaledwie 42 kobiety są prezeskami.

2
00:00:07,086 --> 00:00:08,796
Jakby przyjrzeć się innym państwom,

3
00:00:08,838 --> 00:00:11,424
sytuacja wygląda podobnie, a nawet gorzej.

4
00:00:11,883 --> 00:00:13,718
Oczywiście jest wiele czynników,

5
00:00:13,760 --> 00:00:16,971
które wpływają na ogólnoświatowy brak równości płci w miejscu pracy.

6
00:00:16,971 --> 00:00:20,683
Uważam jednak, że jeden z nich często pozostaje niezauważony:

7
00:00:20,725 --> 00:00:22,268
menopauza.

8
00:00:22,852 --> 00:00:24,187
Co mam na myśli?

9
00:00:24,479 --> 00:00:27,273
Wiele kobiet awansuje na pozycje menadżerskie

10
00:00:27,315 --> 00:00:29,233
po czterdziestce albo po pięćdziesiątce.

11
00:00:29,233 --> 00:00:31,778
Średnia wieku na stanowisku prezesa to 55.

12
00:00:32,111 --> 00:00:35,323
Okres przejściowy przed menopauzą, zwany perimenopauzą,

13
00:00:35,364 --> 00:00:39,327
na ogół występuje między 45. a 55. rokiem życia.

14
00:00:39,494 --> 00:00:41,370
Wtedy pojawiają się objawy,

15
00:00:41,412 --> 00:00:43,289
które mogą się utrzymać przez 10 lat.

16
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335
Więc w momencie, gdy kobieta wchodzi na szczyt kariery

17
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,128
i osiąga adekwatne zarobki,

18
00:00:49,170 --> 00:00:51,255
jej ciało praktycznie ją zdradza.

19
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:54,926
Dla tych, którzy nie wiedzą, a to pewnie większość z nas...

20
00:00:54,967 --> 00:00:58,304
sama odbyłam z mamą pierwszą rozmowę o menopauzie

21
00:00:58,429 --> 00:00:59,889
parę lat temu,

22
00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,432
to poważna sprawa.

23
00:01:01,641 --> 00:01:04,560
Nie tylko dla kobiet po 45. roku życia.

24
00:01:04,644 --> 00:01:07,563
Osoby niebinarne, transpłciowe i młode kobiety,

25
00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233
mogą zmagać się z objawami menopauzy.

26
00:01:10,733 --> 00:01:12,360
Jakie one są?

27
00:01:12,985 --> 00:01:14,195
Mogą być fizyczne:

28
00:01:14,237 --> 00:01:20,159
uderzenia gorąca, bóle stawów, nietrzymanie moczu, obfite miesiączki.

29
00:01:20,201 --> 00:01:23,746
Mogą być psychiczne: niepokój, depresja,

30
00:01:23,788 --> 00:01:26,833
niska samoocena, zaburzenia snu.

31
00:01:26,916 --> 00:01:30,211
Wyobraźcie to sobie - 10 lat problemów ze snem.

32
00:01:31,129 --> 00:01:34,257
Lista jest długa, a objawy mogą się zmienić,

33
00:01:34,298 --> 00:01:36,592
przez co nigdy nie wiadomo, czego się spodziewać.

34
00:01:37,051 --> 00:01:39,470
Są one jednak niezwykle ważne.

35
00:01:39,554 --> 00:01:42,515
Na tyle, że, jak wykazało badanie przeprowadzone przez Vodafone

36
00:01:42,557 --> 00:01:43,933
w pięciu krajach,

37
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,020
60% kobiet zmagających się z objawami menopauzy

38
00:01:48,062 --> 00:01:50,106
przyznało, że wpłynęło to na ich pracę.

39
00:01:50,314 --> 00:01:53,151
Według innego badania, przeprowadzonego w Wielkiej Brytanii,

40
00:01:53,151 --> 00:01:56,821
30% kobiet nie poszło do pracy ze względu na występujące objawy.

41
00:01:57,196 --> 00:02:01,367
Są przypadki, gdy kobiety zmuszone są podjąć poważne decyzje odnośnie kariery.

42
00:02:01,409 --> 00:02:04,829
Nawet 11% zrezygnowało z możliwości awansu,

43
00:02:04,871 --> 00:02:08,166
a 8% zrezygnowało ze swoich stanowisk

44
00:02:08,249 --> 00:02:10,209
z powodu występujących objawów menopauzy.

45
00:02:10,585 --> 00:02:14,297
Moja fascynacja kierownictwem kobiet i oczywistą przepaścią,

46
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:17,175
zaczęła się, gdy pracowałam jako konsultantka ds. strategii

47
00:02:17,175 --> 00:02:18,593
w zarządach na całym świecie.

48
00:02:18,593 --> 00:02:21,179
Na palcach jednej ręki zliczę, ile, przez ponad 10 lat,

49
00:02:21,179 --> 00:02:25,892
widziałam kobiet po pięćdziesiątce na stanowiskach kierowniczych.

50
00:02:26,017 --> 00:02:29,770
Czy to w Seattle, czy w Dubaju, Lagos albo na północy stanu Nowy Jork,

51
00:02:29,812 --> 00:02:31,731
kobiety były rzadkim widokiem u szczytu.

52
00:02:32,565 --> 00:02:35,860
Zaczęłam się rozglądać i kwestionować system, w którym się znalazłam,

53
00:02:35,902 --> 00:02:38,487
zastanawiać się, jakie były rzeczywiste możliwości.

54
00:02:38,571 --> 00:02:41,157
Kobiety na szczycie były wyjątkowe.

55
00:02:41,199 --> 00:02:43,492
Poprzeczka wydawała się niemal nieosiągalna.

56
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:46,662
Zaczęłam rozmawiać z przyjaciółkami i współpracowniczkami

57
00:02:46,704 --> 00:02:48,247
po 40. i 50. roku życia.

58
00:02:48,456 --> 00:02:52,793
Mówiły o znanych mi kwestiach: godzeniu kariery, macierzyństwie

59
00:02:52,835 --> 00:02:54,879
i opiece nad starszymi rodzicami.

60
00:02:55,004 --> 00:02:58,382
W paru przypadkach bliższe mi przyjaciółki otworzyły się bardziej.

61
00:02:58,424 --> 00:03:01,552
Opowiedziały o zmianach zachodzących w ich ciałach i umysłach,

62
00:03:01,552 --> 00:03:05,097
i o tym, jak menopauza wpływa na różne aspekty ich życia,

63
00:03:05,139 --> 00:03:06,349
w tym pracę.

64
00:03:07,391 --> 00:03:09,435
Wtedy zrozumiałam,

65
00:03:09,477 --> 00:03:12,063
że chcąc zmienić sytuację kobiet w miejscu pracy,

66
00:03:12,146 --> 00:03:14,774
żeby każda z nas mogła pracować tak długo, jak chce,

67
00:03:15,274 --> 00:03:17,818
musimy bardziej dbać o kobiety przechodzące menopauzę.

68
00:03:18,110 --> 00:03:20,613
Uważam, że miejsce pracy może odegrać kluczową rolę

69
00:03:20,655 --> 00:03:24,200
w poprawieniu doświadczeń kobiet mających menopauzę.

70
00:03:24,533 --> 00:03:27,828
Może brzmi to banalnie, ale zaczyna się od świadomości.

71
00:03:28,120 --> 00:03:31,582
Potem oznacza to dostosowanie rozłożenia biura,

72
00:03:31,791 --> 00:03:34,252
zmianę oczekiwań odnośnie kultury pracy

73
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,462
i zaktualizowanie strategii opieki zdrowotnej.

74
00:03:36,671 --> 00:03:38,756
A więc, po pierwsze: świadomość.

75
00:03:38,798 --> 00:03:42,468
Jako że temat menopauzy nie jest poruszany ani w szkole,

76
00:03:42,593 --> 00:03:44,762
ani w wiadomościach, ani w popkulturze,

77
00:03:44,804 --> 00:03:48,849
nie można oczekiwać, że nawet pełen dobrych chęci menadżer

78
00:03:48,891 --> 00:03:51,143
będzie wiedział, jak okazać wsparcie.

79
00:03:51,644 --> 00:03:56,107
Ogrom społeczności wywiera na kobietach presję, by nieustannie wyglądały

80
00:03:56,148 --> 00:03:59,235
młodo, szczęśliwie i pełne energii.

81
00:03:59,443 --> 00:04:02,530
Wysiłek włożony w udawanie, że nie przechodzisz

82
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,407
przez naturalny proces starzenia,

83
00:04:04,573 --> 00:04:06,659
jest zupełną stratą czasu.

84
00:04:07,159 --> 00:04:10,955
Tymczasem na dzień kobiety przed lub po menopauzie,

85
00:04:10,997 --> 00:04:14,500
składa się bieganie do łazienki, żeby przeczekać uderzenie ciepła,

86
00:04:14,542 --> 00:04:17,712
szukanie papierowych ręczników na pot, który pojawił się znikąd,

87
00:04:17,753 --> 00:04:19,547
gdy wszyscy inni zamarzają,

88
00:04:19,630 --> 00:04:22,091
pomijanie spotkania lub wysyłanie na nie zamiennika,

89
00:04:22,133 --> 00:04:25,261
bo przez napływ niepokoju czujesz, że sobie nie poradzisz,

90
00:04:25,386 --> 00:04:27,596
albo rezygnacja z wyjazdu służbowego,

91
00:04:27,638 --> 00:04:31,600
bo z obfitymi miesiączkami nie sposób wyjechać z domu.

92
00:04:32,143 --> 00:04:36,480
Sposobem na zwiększenie świadomości jest poruszenie tematu w miejscu pracy.

93
00:04:36,522 --> 00:04:39,066
Wiele firm oferuje programy szkoleniowe

94
00:04:39,108 --> 00:04:41,360
w kwestii różnorodności i inkluzywności,

95
00:04:41,402 --> 00:04:42,862
przeciwdziałania molestowaniu,

96
00:04:42,903 --> 00:04:45,573
rozmowy o zdrowiu psychicznym i urlopie rodzicielskim.

97
00:04:45,990 --> 00:04:48,659
Znormalizujmy rozmowy o menopauzie,

98
00:04:48,701 --> 00:04:51,203
zapraszając ludzi wszelkich płci i w różnym wieku,

99
00:04:51,245 --> 00:04:55,124
aby zrozumieć, co się dzieje podczas tego naturalnego procesu,

100
00:04:55,207 --> 00:04:57,543
żeby ludzie wiedzieli, jak okazać wsparcie.

101
00:04:58,711 --> 00:05:03,299
Drugim krokiem jest rozplanowanie biura i oczekiwania wobec kultury pracy.

102
00:05:03,466 --> 00:05:08,554
Przykładowo, otwarta przestrzeń biurowa jest porażką na każdym kroku,

103
00:05:08,637 --> 00:05:10,723
bo nie można kontrolować temperatury,

104
00:05:10,765 --> 00:05:13,559
nie ma drzwi, które można zamknąć w razie uderzenia gorąca

105
00:05:13,601 --> 00:05:15,436
lub potrzeby chwili na zebranie myśli.

106
00:05:15,853 --> 00:05:19,023
Wiadomo, że nie da się przearanżować całego piętra w jeden dzień.

107
00:05:19,106 --> 00:05:21,567
Ale jest pewnie kilka rzeczy, które można zapewnić:

108
00:05:21,567 --> 00:05:25,404
wiatraczki biurkowe, produkty menstruacyjne,

109
00:05:25,488 --> 00:05:29,033
pozwolenie, na postawienie biurka z dala od kaloryfera,

110
00:05:29,241 --> 00:05:32,453
albo wydzielenie paru pomieszczeń z drzwiami, które można zamknąć,

111
00:05:32,495 --> 00:05:34,205
w razie potrzeby chwili dla siebie.

112
00:05:34,246 --> 00:05:35,706
Są to bardzo dobre pomysły.

113
00:05:36,123 --> 00:05:39,293
W sytuacji, gdy kobieta nosi strój służbowy,

114
00:05:39,418 --> 00:05:41,504
korzystanie z cieńszych materiałów,

115
00:05:41,545 --> 00:05:44,298
które zapewnią przewiewność i odprowadzanie potu,

116
00:05:44,340 --> 00:05:46,759
może stanowić ogromną różnicę dla komfortu kobiety,

117
00:05:46,842 --> 00:05:49,136
tak, jak posiadanie w zanadrzu kilku egzemplarzy,

118
00:05:49,178 --> 00:05:51,514
w razie konieczności przebrania się w ciągu dnia.

119
00:05:51,555 --> 00:05:53,766
Osobom pracującym zdalnie

120
00:05:53,849 --> 00:05:57,603
pomoże podanie kilku przykładów, co powiedzieć przełożonemu,

121
00:05:57,645 --> 00:05:59,397
gdy objawy wymkną się spod kontroli.

122
00:05:59,438 --> 00:06:02,858
Przykładowo: “Ze względu na objawy mam dzisiaj bardzo ciężki dzień,

123
00:06:02,900 --> 00:06:06,362
przez co nie mogę się w pełni zaangażować w pracę“.

124
00:06:06,821 --> 00:06:08,656
A jeśli nie jest to możliwe,

125
00:06:08,781 --> 00:06:10,574
możliwość powiedzenia:

126
00:06:10,658 --> 00:06:15,204
“Dzisiaj potrzebuję paru częstszych przerw zamiast jednej długiej”

127
00:06:15,329 --> 00:06:19,375
albo “Poczekaj chwilę, właśnie mam uderzenie gorąca”.

128
00:06:19,792 --> 00:06:23,629
Uważam, że większa otwartość w tym temacie,

129
00:06:23,671 --> 00:06:27,883
jak i wyrozumiałość biura w kwestii planowania i częstszych przerw,

130
00:06:27,967 --> 00:06:30,678
będzie miała ogromny wpływ na zatrzymywanie kobiet w pracy

131
00:06:30,719 --> 00:06:33,764
i wspieranie ich pomimo przeszkody stojącej na drodze do awansu.

132
00:06:33,806 --> 00:06:35,224
Po trzecie: opieka zdrowotna,

133
00:06:35,266 --> 00:06:36,976
która zdaje się być najważniejsza.

134
00:06:37,059 --> 00:06:38,060
W wielu przypadkach

135
00:06:38,102 --> 00:06:41,730
miejsce pracy zapewni częściową lub pełną opiekę zdrowotną swoim pracownikom.

136
00:06:41,772 --> 00:06:44,775
To okazja, żeby uwzględnić menopauzę.

137
00:06:44,984 --> 00:06:48,320
Kobietom z pewnością pomoże dostęp do rzetelnych informacji

138
00:06:48,362 --> 00:06:50,406
o możliwych opcjach leczenia

139
00:06:50,448 --> 00:06:53,242
oraz lekarzach i specjalistach, z którymi mogą się spotkać:

140
00:06:53,284 --> 00:06:55,953
ginekologach, endokrynologach,

141
00:06:55,995 --> 00:06:58,831
fizjoterapeutach uroginekologicznych albo psychoterapeutach.

142
00:06:58,914 --> 00:07:03,252
Menopauza to bardzo złożony i indywidualny stan,

143
00:07:03,419 --> 00:07:06,213
podczas którego dostęp do wysokiej klasy specjalistów,

144
00:07:06,297 --> 00:07:08,507
z którymi można porozmawiać o sposobach kuracji

145
00:07:08,549 --> 00:07:10,759
i rozważyć najlepszą opcję,

146
00:07:10,926 --> 00:07:12,011
jest kluczowy.

147
00:07:12,386 --> 00:07:14,763
W wielu przypadkach pokrywa to ubezpieczenie

148
00:07:14,805 --> 00:07:17,892
albo dostępne jest w ramach narodowego systemu opieki zdrowotnej,

149
00:07:17,892 --> 00:07:19,768
ale trudno może być się w tym odnaleźć,

150
00:07:19,810 --> 00:07:23,063
a wszystkie objawy tylko wydłużają znalezienie odpowiedniego lekarza

151
00:07:23,105 --> 00:07:24,440
i pozbawiają energii.

152
00:07:24,899 --> 00:07:26,901
Tak, jak mamy poradniki

153
00:07:26,942 --> 00:07:29,195
o tym, czego się spodziewać po urodzeniu dziecka

154
00:07:29,236 --> 00:07:30,613
i jak być dobrym rodzicem,

155
00:07:30,654 --> 00:07:34,450
tak potrzebujemy poradników w kwestii perimenopauzy

156
00:07:34,492 --> 00:07:37,077
i tego, jak okazać wsparcie przez cały okres tranzycji.

157
00:07:37,244 --> 00:07:38,954
Każde miejsce jest inne

158
00:07:39,121 --> 00:07:42,833
i nie wszystkie firmy będą równie przyjaźnie nastawione do zmian.

159
00:07:43,334 --> 00:07:44,710
Ale jak by nie było,

160
00:07:44,752 --> 00:07:49,381
zwiększanie świadomości i okazywanie prawdziwej empatii dużo nie kosztuje.

161
00:07:49,840 --> 00:07:53,469
Nawet w tym roku kilka firm ogłosiło,

162
00:07:53,552 --> 00:07:56,847
że zwiększy liczbę kobiet na wysokich stanowiskach - i wspaniale.

163
00:07:56,889 --> 00:08:00,017
Aby to osiągnąć, konieczna będzie duża doza zaplanowanych działań.

164
00:08:00,017 --> 00:08:01,101
Pierwszy krok?

165
00:08:01,519 --> 00:08:03,479
Traktować menopauzę poważnie.

166
00:08:04,271 --> 00:08:05,439
Dziękuję.
en_source:
1
00:00:01,873 --> 00:00:07,045
Of America's 500 largest companies, only 42 have female CEOs.

2
00:00:07,086 --> 00:00:08,796
And if you look at other countries,

3
00:00:08,838 --> 00:00:11,424
the data is similar, and in some cases, worse.

4
00:00:11,924 --> 00:00:14,052
And, of course, there are a number of factors

5
00:00:14,052 --> 00:00:16,929
contributing to global gender inequity in the workplace.

6
00:00:16,971 --> 00:00:20,683
But I believe that there is one factor often underecognized,

7
00:00:20,725 --> 00:00:22,268
and that's menopause.

8
00:00:22,894 --> 00:00:24,479
So what do I mean?

9
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:27,315
Many women will rise into senior positions of leadership

10
00:00:27,356 --> 00:00:29,192
in their 40s or their 50s.

11
00:00:29,192 --> 00:00:31,736
The average age of a CEO is mid-50s.

12
00:00:32,153 --> 00:00:35,364
Perimenopause, or the transition into menopause,

13
00:00:35,406 --> 00:00:39,535
generally occurs also between 45 and 55.

14
00:00:39,535 --> 00:00:41,621
And this is where the symptoms really start,

15
00:00:41,621 --> 00:00:43,498
and it can last up to 10 years.

16
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335
So just as a woman is stepping up, taking the reins,

17
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,170
earning at her highest potential,

18
00:00:49,212 --> 00:00:51,297
her body can feel like it's betraying her.

19
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:55,134
Now, for anyone who doesn't know, which turns out to be many of us --

20
00:00:55,176 --> 00:00:58,471
for me, I had my first conversation with my own mother about menopause

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:00,056
in the last few years --

22
00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,724
it's a big deal.

23
00:01:01,724 --> 00:01:04,685
And it's not just a big deal for women over 45.

24
00:01:04,685 --> 00:01:07,605
Nonbinary, transmasculine and younger women

25
00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233
may all cope with menopausal symptoms.

26
00:01:10,775 --> 00:01:12,401
So what are these symptoms?

27
00:01:13,027 --> 00:01:14,237
They can be physical,

28
00:01:14,237 --> 00:01:20,201
so that's hot flushes, joint pain, urinary incontinence, heavy periods.

29
00:01:20,201 --> 00:01:23,746
They can also be mental: anxiety, depression,

30
00:01:23,788 --> 00:01:26,916
bouts of low confidence, difficulty sleeping.

31
00:01:26,958 --> 00:01:30,253
Imagine that -- 10 years of difficulty sleeping.

32
00:01:31,129 --> 00:01:34,257
The list is long and symptoms can change,

33
00:01:34,298 --> 00:01:36,592
so it means you never really know what to expect.

34
00:01:37,051 --> 00:01:39,554
But make no mistake: they are significant.

35
00:01:39,554 --> 00:01:44,058
So significant, that in a study by Vodafone across five countries,

36
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,062
it found that 60 percent of women dealing with menopausal symptoms

37
00:01:48,062 --> 00:01:50,314
said it impacted their work.

38
00:01:50,356 --> 00:01:52,608
In another study in the UK,

39
00:01:52,650 --> 00:01:57,238
30 percent of women said they missed work due to their symptoms.

40
00:01:57,280 --> 00:02:01,450
And at the extremes, women are making serious choices about their careers.

41
00:02:01,450 --> 00:02:04,871
As many as 11 percent forewent a promotion opportunity,

42
00:02:04,912 --> 00:02:08,207
and as many as eight percent resigned from their positions

43
00:02:08,249 --> 00:02:10,126
because of menopausal symptoms.

44
00:02:10,585 --> 00:02:14,380
My fascination with female leadership and the obvious gap

45
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:18,259
began as a strategy consultant working in boardrooms around the world.

46
00:02:18,301 --> 00:02:20,928
In over 10 years, I can count on one hand

47
00:02:20,970 --> 00:02:25,975
the number of senior women over 50 in executive positions that I saw,

48
00:02:26,017 --> 00:02:29,770
whether it was Seattle or Dubai, Lagos or upstate New York,

49
00:02:29,812 --> 00:02:31,397
women were scarce at the top.

50
00:02:32,565 --> 00:02:35,860
I started to look around and question the system I was in,

51
00:02:35,902 --> 00:02:38,529
wonder what opportunities really existed.

52
00:02:38,571 --> 00:02:41,199
The women at the top were exceptional.

53
00:02:41,199 --> 00:02:43,492
The bar seemed almost too high to reach.

54
00:02:43,993 --> 00:02:48,456
And so I started speaking to my friends and colleagues in their 40s and 50s.

55
00:02:48,456 --> 00:02:52,793
They talked about things I knew: balancing a serious job, teenagers

56
00:02:52,835 --> 00:02:55,004
and caring for elderly parents.

57
00:02:55,004 --> 00:02:58,382
But in a few instances, close friends opened up.

58
00:02:58,424 --> 00:03:01,552
They talked about how their bodies and their minds were changing

59
00:03:01,552 --> 00:03:05,097
and the impact of menopause on many aspects of their lives,

60
00:03:05,139 --> 00:03:06,349
including work.

61
00:03:07,391 --> 00:03:09,435
It was at that point that I realized

62
00:03:09,477 --> 00:03:12,188
if I wanted to make a difference to women in the workplace

63
00:03:12,230 --> 00:03:15,274
so we can all work as long as we want to,

64
00:03:15,274 --> 00:03:17,151
it was to improve menopause care.

65
00:03:18,110 --> 00:03:20,613
I believe the workplace can play a major role

66
00:03:20,655 --> 00:03:24,533
in improving the experience for women coping with menopausal symptoms.

67
00:03:24,533 --> 00:03:28,120
And it might sound cliché, but it does start with awareness.

68
00:03:28,162 --> 00:03:31,791
And then it means changing the physical setup of our offices,

69
00:03:31,832 --> 00:03:34,293
resetting expectations around work culture

70
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,254
and updating health care policies.

71
00:03:36,671 --> 00:03:38,756
So, first: awareness.

72
00:03:38,798 --> 00:03:42,593
Since we learn virtually nothing about menopause in school,

73
00:03:42,593 --> 00:03:44,762
in the news, in pop culture,

74
00:03:44,804 --> 00:03:48,849
it's impossible to expect that even the most well-intentioned manager

75
00:03:48,891 --> 00:03:51,143
would have a clue how to be supportive.

76
00:03:51,644 --> 00:03:57,358
So many societies place so much pressure on women to appear young and happy

77
00:03:57,400 --> 00:03:59,443
and vibrant all of the time.

78
00:03:59,443 --> 00:04:02,822
All that effort put towards pretending that you're not going through

79
00:04:02,822 --> 00:04:04,532
a natural process of aging

80
00:04:04,573 --> 00:04:06,659
is a complete waste of time.

81
00:04:07,159 --> 00:04:10,955
Meanwhile, a day in the life of a peri- or postmenopausal woman

82
00:04:10,997 --> 00:04:14,500
could be running to the bathroom to wait out a hot flush,

83
00:04:14,542 --> 00:04:17,753
looking for paper towels for perspiration that came out of nowhere

84
00:04:17,753 --> 00:04:19,672
when everyone else was freezing,

85
00:04:19,672 --> 00:04:22,049
skipping a meeting or sending someone else,

86
00:04:22,049 --> 00:04:25,386
because a wave of anxiety made you feel like you couldn't cope

87
00:04:25,386 --> 00:04:27,596
or skipping a business trip altogether,

88
00:04:27,638 --> 00:04:31,600
because heavy periods made leaving home virtually impossible.

89
00:04:32,143 --> 00:04:36,480
One way to raise awareness is to bring the discussion right into the workplace.

90
00:04:36,522 --> 00:04:39,066
Many companies already offer training programs

91
00:04:39,108 --> 00:04:41,444
on things like diversity and inclusion,

92
00:04:41,485 --> 00:04:42,737
anti-harassment,

93
00:04:42,737 --> 00:04:45,573
conversations on mental health or parental leave.

94
00:04:46,032 --> 00:04:48,659
Let's normalize conversation on menopause,

95
00:04:48,701 --> 00:04:51,245
inviting people of all genders and all ages

96
00:04:51,245 --> 00:04:55,249
to understand what's happening in this natural process of aging,

97
00:04:55,249 --> 00:04:57,585
so people can learn how to be supportive.

98
00:04:58,711 --> 00:05:03,424
Next is the physical setup and expectations around work culture.

99
00:05:03,466 --> 00:05:08,637
For example, open-plan offices are a disaster for so many reasons,

100
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,765
not being able to control the temperature,

101
00:05:10,806 --> 00:05:13,601
having no doors to close when you're having a hot flush

102
00:05:13,642 --> 00:05:15,853
or need a moment to regroup.

103
00:05:15,853 --> 00:05:19,065
And, of course, you can't change an entire floor plan overnight.

104
00:05:19,106 --> 00:05:21,567
But there probably are some things you can do,

105
00:05:21,567 --> 00:05:25,488
whether that's desk fans, availability of period products,

106
00:05:25,488 --> 00:05:29,200
letting someone move their desk from closest to the radiator

107
00:05:29,241 --> 00:05:32,495
or having a few rooms with a few doors that you can close

108
00:05:32,495 --> 00:05:34,205
if you need a moment to regroup

109
00:05:34,205 --> 00:05:36,123
are all good ideas.

110
00:05:36,123 --> 00:05:39,418
In situations where women wear uniforms at work,

111
00:05:39,418 --> 00:05:41,545
updating the cut to a thinner fabric,

112
00:05:41,545 --> 00:05:44,298
making it more breathable or even sweat-wicking

113
00:05:44,340 --> 00:05:46,842
can make a huge difference to a woman's comfort,

114
00:05:46,884 --> 00:05:48,677
as well as having a few extra around,

115
00:05:48,719 --> 00:05:50,888
if a quick change is required during the day.

116
00:05:51,555 --> 00:05:53,849
For employees who can work remotely,

117
00:05:53,891 --> 00:05:57,686
you can make it easier by giving examples of things to say to a manager

118
00:05:57,686 --> 00:05:59,271
when symptoms are out of control.

119
00:05:59,313 --> 00:06:02,900
For example, "I'm having a really tough day due to my symptoms,

120
00:06:02,900 --> 00:06:06,821
and it's making it difficult for me to perform at my best in the office."

121
00:06:06,862 --> 00:06:08,739
In cases where that's not possible,

122
00:06:08,781 --> 00:06:10,658
being able to say things like,

123
00:06:10,699 --> 00:06:15,329
"I need to take a few more frequent breaks today rather than one long break,"

124
00:06:15,329 --> 00:06:19,792
or "Hold on a second -- I'm having a hot flush."

125
00:06:19,834 --> 00:06:23,671
I truly believe that more openness about this topic,

126
00:06:23,671 --> 00:06:27,967
as well as leniency from an office around scheduling or more frequent breaks

127
00:06:28,008 --> 00:06:30,678
will make a huge difference to retaining women

128
00:06:30,678 --> 00:06:33,055
and getting them over that next promotion hurdle.

129
00:06:33,806 --> 00:06:35,224
And then there's health care,

130
00:06:35,224 --> 00:06:37,017
which is perhaps the most important.

131
00:06:37,059 --> 00:06:38,227
In many cases,

132
00:06:38,227 --> 00:06:41,689
a workplace will provide in-part or in-full health care to its employees.

133
00:06:41,730 --> 00:06:44,984
So there's an opportunity to include menopause directly.

134
00:06:44,984 --> 00:06:48,320
It can benefit a woman to have access to real information

135
00:06:48,362 --> 00:06:50,406
about what treatment options are available

136
00:06:50,448 --> 00:06:53,200
and what doctors or specialists she may want to see,

137
00:06:53,242 --> 00:06:55,953
whether that's gynecologists, endocrinologists,

138
00:06:55,995 --> 00:06:57,204
pelvic floor physios

139
00:06:57,204 --> 00:06:58,873
or talk therapists.

140
00:06:58,914 --> 00:07:03,461
Menopause is a highly complex and individualized situation,

141
00:07:03,461 --> 00:07:06,255
and having access to high-quality health care providers

142
00:07:06,297 --> 00:07:08,591
that can talk you through your treatment options

143
00:07:08,632 --> 00:07:10,926
and help you figure out what you want to do

144
00:07:10,926 --> 00:07:12,386
is critical.

145
00:07:12,386 --> 00:07:14,805
In many cases, insurance does cover this,

146
00:07:14,847 --> 00:07:17,850
or national health systems do have this available,

147
00:07:17,892 --> 00:07:19,477
but it can be hard to navigate,

148
00:07:19,518 --> 00:07:23,063
and all of the symptoms make finding the right doctor time-consuming

149
00:07:23,105 --> 00:07:24,857
and energy-draining.

150
00:07:24,899 --> 00:07:26,901
Just as we have health care resources

151
00:07:26,942 --> 00:07:30,613
for what to expect when you're having a child and how to be a great parent,

152
00:07:30,654 --> 00:07:34,492
we need better health care resources for what to expect in perimenopause

153
00:07:34,492 --> 00:07:36,702
and support through the entire transition.

154
00:07:37,244 --> 00:07:39,079
Every workplace is different,

155
00:07:39,121 --> 00:07:42,958
and not all companies will have the same approach to becoming menopause friendly.

156
00:07:43,334 --> 00:07:44,710
But at the very least,

157
00:07:44,752 --> 00:07:49,465
increasing awareness and demonstrating some real empathy can be low-cost.

158
00:07:49,882 --> 00:07:53,511
Just this year, we've heard several corporations make announcements

159
00:07:53,552 --> 00:07:56,847
about boosting female leadership at the top -- and that's great.

160
00:07:56,889 --> 00:07:59,683
And it will take a lot of coordinated efforts to get there.

161
00:07:59,725 --> 00:08:01,560
But what's the first step?

162
00:08:01,602 --> 00:08:03,771
Getting serious about menopause.

163
00:08:04,313 --> 00:08:05,564
Thank you.

15
00:00:41,621 --> 00:00:43,498
and it can last up to 10 years.

16
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335
So just as a woman is stepping up, taking the reins,

17
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,170
earning at her highest potential,

18
00:00:49,212 --> 00:00:51,297
her body can feel like it's betraying her.

19
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:55,134
Now, for anyone who doesn't know, which turns out to be many of us --

20
00:00:55,176 --> 00:00:58,471
for me, I had my first conversation with my own mother about menopause

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:00,056
in the last few years --

22
00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,724
it's a big deal.

23
00:01:01,724 --> 00:01:04,685
And it's not just a big deal for women over 45.

24
00:01:04,685 --> 00:01:07,605
Nonbinary, transmasculine and younger women

25
00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233
may all cope with menopausal symptoms.

26
00:01:10,775 --> 00:01:12,401
So what are these symptoms?

27
00:01:13,027 --> 00:01:14,237
They can be physical,

28
00:01:14,237 --> 00:01:20,201
so that's hot flushes, joint pain, urinary incontinence, heavy periods.

29
00:01:20,201 --> 00:01:23,746
They can also be mental: anxiety, depression,

30
00:01:23,788 --> 00:01:26,916
bouts of low confidence, difficulty sleeping.

31
00:01:26,958 --> 00:01:30,253
Imagine that -- 10 years of difficulty sleeping.

32
00:01:31,129 --> 00:01:34,257
The list is long and symptoms can change,

33
00:01:34,298 --> 00:01:36,592
so it means you never really know what to expect.

34
00:01:37,051 --> 00:01:39,554
But make no mistake: they are significant.

35
00:01:39,554 --> 00:01:44,058
So significant, that in a study by Vodafone across five countries,

36
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,062
it found that 60 percent of women dealing with menopausal symptoms

37
00:01:48,062 --> 00:01:50,314
said it impacted their work.

38
00:01:50,356 --> 00:01:52,608
In another study in the UK,

39
00:01:52,650 --> 00:01:57,238
30 percent of women said they missed work due to their symptoms.

40
00:01:57,280 --> 00:02:01,450
And at the extremes, women are making serious choices about their careers.

41
00:02:01,450 --> 00:02:04,871
As many as 11 percent forewent a promotion opportunity,

42
00:02:04,912 --> 00:02:08,207
and as many as eight percent resigned from their positions

43
00:02:08,249 --> 00:02:10,126
because of menopausal symptoms.

44
00:02:10,585 --> 00:02:14,380
My fascination with female leadership and the obvious gap

45
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:18,259
began as a strategy consultant working in boardrooms around the world.

46
00:02:18,301 --> 00:02:20,928
In over 10 years, I can count on one hand

47
00:02:20,970 --> 00:02:25,975
the number of senior women over 50 in executive positions that I saw,

48
00:02:26,017 --> 00:02:29,770
whether it was Seattle or Dubai, Lagos or upstate New York,

49
00:02:29,812 --> 00:02:31,397
women were scarce at the top.

50
00:02:32,565 --> 00:02:35,860
I started to look around and question the system I was in,

51
00:02:35,902 --> 00:02:38,529
wonder what opportunities really existed.

52
00:02:38,571 --> 00:02:41,199
The women at the top were exceptional.

53
00:02:41,199 --> 00:02:43,492
The bar seemed almost too high to reach.

54
00:02:43,993 --> 00:02:48,456
And so I started speaking to my friends and colleagues in their 40s and 50s.

55
00:02:48,456 --> 00:02:52,793
They talked about things I knew: balancing a serious job, teenagers

56
00:02:52,835 --> 00:02:55,004
and caring for elderly parents.

57
00:02:55,004 --> 00:02:58,382
But in a few instances, close friends opened up.

58
00:02:58,424 --> 00:03:01,552
They talked about how their bodies and their minds were changing

59
00:03:01,552 --> 00:03:05,097
and the impact of menopause on many aspects of their lives,

60
00:03:05,139 --> 00:03:06,349
including work.

61
00:03:07,391 --> 00:03:09,435
It was at that point that I realized

62
00:03:09,477 --> 00:03:12,188
if I wanted to make a difference to women in the workplace

63
00:03:12,230 --> 00:03:15,274
so we can all work as long as we want to,

64
00:03:15,274 --> 00:03:17,151
it was to improve menopause care.

65
00:03:18,110 --> 00:03:20,613
I believe the workplace can play a major role

66
00:03:20,655 --> 00:03:24,533
in improving the experience for women coping with menopausal symptoms.

67
00:03:24,533 --> 00:03:28,120
And it might sound cliché, but it does start with awareness.

68
00:03:28,162 --> 00:03:31,791
And then it means changing the physical setup of our offices,

69
00:03:31,832 --> 00:03:34,293
resetting expectations around work culture

70
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,254
and updating health care policies.

71
00:03:36,671 --> 00:03:38,756
So, first: awareness.

72
00:03:38,798 --> 00:03:42,593
Since we learn virtually nothing about menopause in school,

73
00:03:42,593 --> 00:03:44,762
in the news, in pop culture,

74
00:03:44,804 --> 00:03:48,849
it's impossible to expect that even the most well-intentioned manager

75
00:03:48,891 --> 00:03:51,143
would have a clue how to be supportive.

76
00:03:51,644 --> 00:03:57,358
So many societies place so much pressure on women to appear young and happy

77
00:03:57,400 --> 00:03:59,443
and vibrant all of the time.

78
00:03:59,443 --> 00:04:02,822
All that effort put towards pretending that you're not going through

79
00:04:02,822 --> 00:04:04,532
a natural process of aging

80
00:04:04,573 --> 00:04:06,659
is a complete waste of time.

81
00:04:07,159 --> 00:04:10,955
Meanwhile, a day in the life of a peri- or postmenopausal woman

82
00:04:10,997 --> 00:04:14,500
could be running to the bathroom to wait out a hot flush,

83
00:04:14,542 --> 00:04:17,753
looking for paper towels for perspiration that came out of nowhere

84
00:04:17,753 --> 00:04:19,672
when everyone else was freezing,

85
00:04:19,672 --> 00:04:22,049
skipping a meeting or sending someone else,

86
00:04:22,049 --> 00:04:25,386
because a wave of anxiety made you feel like you couldn't cope

87
00:04:25,386 --> 00:04:27,596
or skipping a business trip altogether,

88
00:04:27,638 --> 00:04:31,600
because heavy periods made leaving home virtually impossible.

89
00:04:32,143 --> 00:04:36,480
One way to raise awareness is to bring the discussion right into the workplace.

90
00:04:36,522 --> 00:04:39,066
Many companies already offer training programs

91
00:04:39,108 --> 00:04:41,444
on things like diversity and inclusion,

92
00:04:41,485 --> 00:04:42,737
anti-harassment,

93
00:04:42,737 --> 00:04:45,573
conversations on mental health or parental leave.

94
00:04:46,032 --> 00:04:48,659
Let's normalize conversation on menopause,

95
00:04:48,701 --> 00:04:51,245
inviting people of all genders and all ages

96
00:04:51,245 --> 00:04:55,249
to understand what's happening in this natural process of aging,

97
00:04:55,249 --> 00:04:57,585
so people can learn how to be supportive.

98
00:04:58,711 --> 00:05:03,424
Next is the physical setup and expectations around work culture.

99
00:05:03,466 --> 00:05:08,637
For example, open-plan offices are a disaster for so many reasons,

100
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,765
not being able to control the temperature,

00:00:01,873 --> 00:00:07,045
W 500 największych amerykańskich firmach, zaledwie 42 kobiety są prezeskami.

00:00:07,086 --> 00:00:08,796
Jeśli przyjrzeć się innym krajom,

A oczywiście, jest wiele czynników,

00:00:14,052 --> 00:00:16,929

6
00:00:16,971 --> 00:00:20,683

7
00:00:20,725 --> 00:00:22,268
a mianowicie: menopauza.

8
00:00:22,894 --> 00:00:24,479
O co tu chodzi?

9
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:27,315
Wiele kobiet dochodzi do seniorów w kariere

10
00:00:27,356 --> 00:00:29,192
w latach 40. i 50.

11
00:00:29,192 --> 00:00:31,736
Średni wiek prezesa to średnia półwiecze.

12
00:00:32,153 --> 00:00:35,364
Przeniałężycie, czyli przejście przez menopauzę,

13
00:00:35,406 --> 00:00:39,535
zwykle również trwa wówczas — między 45. a 55. rokiem życia.

14
00:00:39,535 --> 00:00:41,621

15
00:00:41,621 --> 00:00:43,498

16
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335
Więc właśnie w chwili, gdy kobieta zdobywa pozycję, objmuje odpowiedzialność,

17
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,170

18
00:00:49,212 --> 00:00:51,297

19
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:55,134
Teraz, dla tych, którzy tego nie wiedzą — co okazuje się wielu z nas,

00:00:55,176 --> 00:01:00,056

00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,724

00:01:04,685 --> 00:01:07,605

00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233

Są fizyczne —

title: If trees could speak
summary:
1
00:00:00,876 --> 00:00:03,196
Ludzie nie widzą drzew.

2
00:00:03,236 --> 00:00:05,476
Mijają nas każdego dnia.

3
00:00:05,516 --> 00:00:09,516
Siedzą, śpią, palą, urządzają pikniki,

4
00:00:09,516 --> 00:00:11,916
i całują się ukradkiem w naszym cieniu.

5
00:00:11,916 --> 00:00:15,436
Zrywają nasze liście i rozkoszują się owocami.

6
00:00:15,476 --> 00:00:17,156
Łamią nasze gałęzie

7
00:00:17,196 --> 00:00:21,196
albo drążą ostrzami imiona ukochanych na naszych pniach,

8
00:00:21,236 --> 00:00:23,476
ślubując wieczną miłość.

9
00:00:24,116 --> 00:00:26,596
Wyplatają naszyjniki z naszych igieł

10
00:00:26,636 --> 00:00:29,396
i malują nasze kwiaty na obrazach.

11
00:00:29,396 --> 00:00:32,716
Rąbią nas na kłody, by ogrzać własne domy,

12
00:00:32,756 --> 00:00:35,076
a czasami nas ścinają,

13
00:00:35,116 --> 00:00:38,276
sądząc, że zasłaniamy widok.

14
00:00:38,276 --> 00:00:43,436
Robią z nas kołyski, korki do wina, gumę do żucia, rustykalne meble.

15
00:00:43,476 --> 00:00:46,956
Tworzą z nas najpiękniejszą muzykę.

16
00:00:47,356 --> 00:00:49,236
Zmieniają nas w książki,

17
00:00:49,236 --> 00:00:53,236
z którymi spędzają mroźne, zimowe wieczory.

18
00:00:53,276 --> 00:00:58,596
Z naszego drewna produkują trumny, w których kończą własne życie.

19
00:00:58,636 --> 00:01:02,836
Piszą niezwykle romantyczne wiersze na naszą cześć,

20
00:01:02,876 --> 00:01:07,356
uznając, że łączymy niebo i ziemię.

21
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:09,916
A jednak nie widzą nas.

22
00:01:12,796 --> 00:01:16,516
Pięknem sztuki opowiadania historii

23
00:01:16,516 --> 00:01:20,556
jest wyobrażanie sobie siebie, mówiącego głosem kogoś innego.

24
00:01:20,596 --> 00:01:25,316
Choć uwielbiamy historie i słowa,

25
00:01:25,316 --> 00:01:29,076
to jako pisarze musimy interesować się milczeniem.

26
00:01:29,636 --> 00:01:33,836
Milczeniem kwestii, o których nie możemy rozmawiać swobodnie w społeczeństwie.

27
00:01:33,876 --> 00:01:36,956
O osobach marginalizowanych, pozbawionych władzy.

28
00:01:36,956 --> 00:01:41,036
Pod tym względem literatura może, i miejmy nadzieję, że to robi,

29
00:01:41,036 --> 00:01:43,796
zbliża obrzeża do środka,

30
00:01:43,836 --> 00:01:46,636
sprawia, że niewidzialne zostaje dostrzeżone,

31
00:01:46,676 --> 00:01:49,396
niesłyszalne zostaje usłyszane,

32
00:01:49,436 --> 00:01:55,276
a empatia i zrozumienie są głośniejsze niż demagogia i apatia.

33
00:01:55,956 --> 00:01:58,556
Historie nas łączą.

34
00:01:58,596 --> 00:02:03,556
Nieopowiedziane historie i zakorzenione milczenie nas dzielą.

35
00:02:04,116 --> 00:02:09,476
Ale jak opowiadać historie ludzkości i natury w czasie,

36
00:02:09,476 --> 00:02:11,676
gdy nasza planeta płonie

37
00:02:11,676 --> 00:02:13,436
i nie ma historycznego wzoru tego,

38
00:02:13,476 --> 00:02:16,556
czego wspólnie doświadczymy

39
00:02:16,556 --> 00:02:20,876
politycznie, społecznie i ekologicznie.

40
00:02:21,516 --> 00:02:22,996
Musimy jednak opowiadać,

41
00:02:23,036 --> 00:02:24,476
bo jeśli istnieje jedna rzecz,

42
00:02:24,516 --> 00:02:27,636
która niszczy nasz świat bardziej niż cokolwiek innego,

43
00:02:27,676 --> 00:02:29,276
to jest nią otępnienie.

44
00:02:29,316 --> 00:02:35,036
Gdy ludzie tracą kontakt, stają się znieczuleni i obojętni,

45
00:02:35,076 --> 00:02:38,516
gdy przestają słuchać, gdy przestają się uczyć

46
00:02:38,556 --> 00:02:40,276
i gdy przestają dbać o to,

47
00:02:40,316 --> 00:02:44,636
co dzieje się tutaj, tam i wszędzie.

48
00:02:48,436 --> 00:02:52,196
Drzewa i ludzie inaczej mierzą czas.

49
00:02:52,236 --> 00:02:54,076
Ludzki czas jest linerany.

50
00:02:54,076 --> 00:02:55,476
To uporządkowana ciągłość,

51
00:02:55,476 --> 00:03:00,196
trwająca od przeszłości, która minęła i nie wróci

52
00:03:00,196 --> 00:03:04,876
do przyszłości, która rzekomo jest czysta i nieskażona.

53
00:03:05,236 --> 00:03:07,276
Czas drzew jest cykliczny.

54
00:03:07,276 --> 00:03:12,076
Zarówno przeszłość, jak i przyszłość oddychają w tej samej chwili.

55
00:03:12,076 --> 00:03:15,516
A teraźniejszość nie porusza się w jednym kierunku.

56
00:03:15,556 --> 00:03:19,156
W zamian, zakreśla koła wewnątrz kół,

57
00:03:19,156 --> 00:03:23,076
jak pierścienie, które zobaczysz, gdy nas zetniesz.

58
00:03:23,796 --> 00:03:28,516
Kolejny raz, gdy będziesz mijał drzewo, spróbuj zwolnić i posłuchać,

59
00:03:28,556 --> 00:03:31,556
bo każde z nas szepcze coś na wietrze.

60
00:03:31,556 --> 00:03:32,756
Spójrz na nas.

61
00:03:32,756 --> 00:03:35,396
Jesteśmy starsze niż ty i twój gatunek.

62
00:03:35,436 --> 00:03:37,756
Posłuchaj, co mamy do powiedzenia,

63
00:03:37,796 --> 00:03:43,716

en_source:
1
00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:03,216
Humans do not see trees.

2
00:00:03,240 --> 00:00:05,476
They walk by us every day.

3
00:00:05,500 --> 00:00:09,507
They sit and sleep, smoke and picnic

4
00:00:09,531 --> 00:00:11,910
and secretly kiss in our shade.

5
00:00:11,934 --> 00:00:15,436
They pluck our leaves and gorge on our fruits.

6
00:00:15,460 --> 00:00:17,166
They break our branches

7
00:00:17,190 --> 00:00:21,206
or carve their lover's name on our trunks with their blades

8
00:00:21,230 --> 00:00:23,493
and vow eternal love.

9
00:00:24,130 --> 00:00:26,616
They weave necklaces out of our needles

10
00:00:26,640 --> 00:00:29,386
and paint our flowers into art.

11
00:00:29,410 --> 00:00:32,726
They split us into logs to heat their homes,

12
00:00:32,750 --> 00:00:35,076
and sometimes they chop us down

13
00:00:35,100 --> 00:00:38,266
just because they think we obstruct their view.

14
00:00:38,290 --> 00:00:43,452
They make cradles, wine corks, chewing gum, rustic furniture

15
00:00:43,476 --> 00:00:46,970
and produce the most beautiful music out of us.

16
00:00:47,360 --> 00:00:49,226
And they turn us into books

17
00:00:49,250 --> 00:00:53,246
in which they bury themselves on cold winter nights.

18
00:00:53,270 --> 00:00:58,606
They use our wood to manufacture coffins in which they end their lives.

19
00:00:58,630 --> 00:01:02,836
And they even compose the most romantic poems for us,

20
00:01:02,860 --> 00:01:07,356
claiming we're the link between earth and sky.

21
00:01:07,380 --> 00:01:09,923
And yet, they do not see us.

22
00:01:12,790 --> 00:01:16,516
So one of the many beauties of the art of storytelling

23
00:01:16,540 --> 00:01:20,566
is to imagine yourself inside someone else's voice.

24
00:01:20,590 --> 00:01:25,306
But as writers, as much as we love stories and words,

25
00:01:25,330 --> 00:01:29,606
I believe we must also be interested in silences:

26
00:01:29,630 --> 00:01:33,836
the things we cannot talk about easily in our societies,

27
00:01:33,860 --> 00:01:36,946
the marginalized, the disempowered.

28
00:01:36,970 --> 00:01:41,026
In that sense, literature can, and hopefully does,

29
00:01:41,050 --> 00:01:43,816
bring the periphery to the center,

30
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:46,636
make the invisible a bit more visible,

31
00:01:46,660 --> 00:01:49,416
make the unheard a bit more heard,

32
00:01:49,440 --> 00:01:55,275
and empathy and understanding speak louder than demagoguery and apathy.

33
00:01:55,974 --> 00:01:58,565
Stories bring us together.

34
00:01:58,589 --> 00:02:03,560
Untold stories and entrenched silences keep us apart.

35
00:02:04,130 --> 00:02:08,606
But how to tell the stories of humanity and nature

36
00:02:08,630 --> 00:02:11,666
at a time when our planet is burning

37
00:02:11,690 --> 00:02:13,456
and there is no precedent

38
00:02:13,480 --> 00:02:16,556
for what we're about to experience collectively

39
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:20,863
whether it's political, social or ecological?

40
00:02:21,500 --> 00:02:23,006
But tell we must

41
00:02:23,030 --> 00:02:24,496
because if there's one thing

42
00:02:24,520 --> 00:02:27,646
that is destroying our world more than anything,

43
00:02:27,670 --> 00:02:29,276
it is numbness.

44
00:02:29,300 --> 00:02:35,056
When people become disconnected, desensitized, indifferent,

45
00:02:35,080 --> 00:02:38,539
when they stop listening, when they stop learning

46
00:02:38,563 --> 00:02:40,296
and when they stop caring

47
00:02:40,320 --> 00:02:44,653
about what's happening here, there and everywhere.

48
00:02:48,430 --> 00:02:52,196
We measure time differently, trees and humans.

49
00:02:52,220 --> 00:02:54,066
Human time is linear --

50
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:55,463
a neat continuum

51
00:02:55,487 --> 00:03:00,271
stretching from a past that is deemed to be over and done with

52
00:03:00,295 --> 00:03:04,860
towards the future that is supposed to be pristine, untouched.

53
00:03:05,240 --> 00:03:07,269
Tree time is circular.

54
00:03:07,293 --> 00:03:12,066
Both the past and the future breathe within the present moment.

55
00:03:12,090 --> 00:03:15,536
And the present does not move in one direction.

56
00:03:15,560 --> 00:03:19,146
Instead it draws circles within circles,

57
00:03:19,170 --> 00:03:23,063
like the rings you would find when you cut us down.

58
00:03:23,790 --> 00:03:28,526
Next time you walk by a tree, try to slow down and listen

59
00:03:28,550 --> 00:03:31,546
because each of us whispers in the wind.

60
00:03:31,570 --> 00:03:32,744
Look at us.

61
00:03:32,768 --> 00:03:35,396
We're older than you and your kind.

62
00:03:35,420 --> 00:03:37,769
Listen to what we have to tell,

63
00:03:37,793 --> 00:03:43,700
because hidden inside our story is the past and the future of humanity.

You are a professional translator. Translate the following English TED Talk
transcript into Polish.

The transcript is in SRT subtitle format. Each cue block consists of:
  1. a cue number
  2. a timestamp line (HH:MM:SS,mmm --> HH:MM:SS,mmm)
  3. one or more lines of text

CRITICAL - completeness (read first):
- You MUST translate EVERY SINGLE cue, from the first to the very last one in the input.
- Your output MUST contain exactly as many cue blocks as the input. Do not stop early.
- NEVER skip, summarize, abbreviate, or collapse cues. Translate each one in full.
- NEVER write meta-comments such as "(the rest is kept as in the original)",
  "(remaining cues continue in the same manner)", "..." or any placeholder in place
  of a real translation. Every cue must contain the actual translated text.
- If the transcript is long, keep going regardless — finish all cues.

Rules:
- Preserve every cue number exactly as it appears
- Preserve every timestamp line exactly as it appears
- Translate only the text lines of each cue
- Keep the same SRT structure (cue number, timestamp line, text) for every cue
- Where appropriate, choose informal, colloquial terms over formal or academic ones.
- Choose modern terms and phrases over traditional ones. Translators should be well-versed in the topics covered.
- Strive to match the tone and flow of the speaker's original talk. Rather than produce a word-for-word translation, aim to find the color, energy and "poetry" in the speaker's organic style and try to emulate it.
- Choose words and phrases that are most universally understood among all dialects.
- Instead of a word-for-word translation, try finding a similar expression in the target language. If no equivalent exists, opt for the translation that readers will find least confusing, even if it is less colorful than the original.
- TED is always written as "TED" and should not be translated.
- For books, movies, magazines and poems, check if the work has an official translation in your language; if not, don't translate the title.
- If the target language uses a non-Latin alphabet, transliterate people's names. For places, use the name that is most common in your language. Otherwise, transliterate.
- Use the target language's native punctuation.
- Use standard unicode characters and avoid those that are platform-specific.
- You may convert units of measurement to make them more understandable to viewers in your language.
- Preserve all stage directions in parentheses, e.g. (Laughter), (Applause)
  — translate them into Polish as well
- Do not translate proper names (people, places, organizations)
- Your final answer must be the translated SRT text ONLY — no introduction, no
  commentary, no explanation before or after it (any reasoning stays out of the answer)

36
00:02:14,916 --> 00:02:17,436
Prawie dwukrotnie zwiększył swoją objętość.

37
00:02:18,036 --> 00:02:23,836
W moim wieku stanowi prawie 90 procent objętości mózgu dorosłego człowieka.

38
00:02:26,396 --> 00:02:29,036
Mózg rozwija się szybciej w pierwszych latach życia

39
00:02:29,276 --> 00:02:32,756
niż na innych jego etapach.

40
00:02:33,636 --> 00:02:38,676
Potrafi stworzyć około miliona połączeń nerwowych na sekundę.

41
00:02:39,476 --> 00:02:41,036
Ale potrzebujemy waszej pomocy.

42
00:02:41,996 --> 00:02:46,396
Nasz zdrowy rozwój zależy od tych pięciu rzeczy:

43
00:02:46,516 --> 00:02:49,996
Po pierwsze: więź pomiędzy nami, po drugie: rozmowa,

44
00:02:50,036 --> 00:02:53,876
po trzecie: zabawa, po czwarte: zdrowy dom,

45
00:02:53,916 --> 00:02:55,836
po piąte: otoczenie.

46
00:02:55,996 --> 00:03:00,236
Wszystko to pomaga naszym mózgom i nam osiągnąć nasz pełny potencjał.

47
00:03:00,516 --> 00:03:04,956
Więc co możecie zrobić, by pozytywnie wpłynąć na nasz rozwój?

48
00:03:05,356 --> 00:03:08,316
Naukowcy nazywają to “akcją - reakcją”.

49
00:03:08,676 --> 00:03:14,356
Tak dorośli mówią na budowanie więzi, rozmowę i zabawę z nami.

50
00:03:14,596 --> 00:03:17,836
A teraz wielkie wydarzenie!

51
00:03:17,876 --> 00:03:19,956
Amarjot, Ari, jesteście gotowi?

52
00:03:19,956 --> 00:03:21,436
(Dźwięk) Amarjot: Gotowy?

53
00:03:21,476 --> 00:03:25,036
Molly Wright: Zabawy w naśladowanie ćwiczą wyobraźnię i empatię.

54
00:03:25,516 --> 00:03:29,436
(Amarjot i Ari śmieją się)

55
00:03:29,716 --> 00:03:33,756
Gry nazewnicze rozwijają słownictwo i uwagę.

56
00:03:33,796 --> 00:03:38,396
Amarjot: Tatuś. Ari. Tatuś. Ari.

57
00:03:38,436 --> 00:03:41,276
MW: A zabawy jak gra w a kuku, tak, w a kuku,

58
00:03:41,316 --> 00:03:43,596
rozwijają pamięć i zaufanie.

59
00:03:44,156 --> 00:03:46,556
Amarjot: A kuku!

60
00:03:46,636 --> 00:03:51,236
MW: Za każdym razem, gdy się z nami bawicie, rozśmieszacie nas,

61
00:03:51,236 --> 00:03:56,556
nie tylko rozwijacie i wzmacniacie nasze relacje i zdrowie psychiczne,

62
00:03:56,596 --> 00:04:00,756
ale uczycie nas najważniejszych umiejętności życiowych,

63
00:04:00,796 --> 00:04:03,716
takich jak zdobywanie przyjaciół, zdawanie egzaminów,

64
00:04:03,756 --> 00:04:04,916
szukanie pracy,

65
00:04:04,956 --> 00:04:08,876
czy może nawet zakładanie rodziny w przyszłości.

66
00:04:09,436 --> 00:04:12,276
Wczesna i częsta interakcja ma znaczenie.

67
00:04:12,316 --> 00:04:13,476
Popatrzcie na mnie:

68
00:04:13,516 --> 00:04:17,036
siedmiolatka mówiąca o mózgu.

69
00:04:17,076 --> 00:04:18,516
(Śmiech)

70
00:04:18,556 --> 00:04:22,916
Teraz zobaczmy, co się stanie, gdy nie będzie interakcji.

71
00:04:25,756 --> 00:04:28,676
Dziecko próbuje przyciągnąć uwagę taty.

72
00:04:28,716 --> 00:04:32,396
Zdaje się mówić: Fajnie się bawiliśmy, czemu się skończyło?

73
00:04:34,596 --> 00:04:36,556
(Ari płacze)

74
00:04:36,596 --> 00:04:41,436
Wiem, że dla dorosłych korzystanie z urządzeń elektronicznych jest ważne,

75
00:04:41,476 --> 00:04:46,316
ale dzieci są tak zaprogramowane, że potrzebują głębokich relacji,

76
00:04:46,356 --> 00:04:50,076
a gdy ich nie otrzymują, doświadczają stresu i poczucia zagubienia.

77
00:04:50,956 --> 00:04:54,076
Dobrze, Amarjot, ponownie zwróć uwagę na dziecko.

78
00:04:54,556 --> 00:04:56,476
Amarjot: Ah, tutaj jesteś Ari.

79
00:04:56,676 --> 00:04:59,236
(Ari śmieje się)

80
00:05:01,436 --> 00:05:07,356
MW: A gdyby tak całe nasze dzieciństwo wyglądało jak te 30 sekund?

81
00:05:07,916 --> 00:05:10,636
Jak trudno byłoby dziecku czuć się spokojnym.

82
00:05:11,676 --> 00:05:13,516
Czuć się bezpiecznym.

83
00:05:14,156 --> 00:05:16,516
Uczyć się zaufania do innych.

84
00:05:16,556 --> 00:05:19,556
Wyobraźcie sobie, jaki miałoby to wpływ na całe nasze życie.

85
00:05:20,676 --> 00:05:22,796
Jest mi smutno, kiedy o tym myślę.

86
00:05:26,796 --> 00:05:31,756
W przypadku Ari’ego była to tylko krótka reakcja, z którą szybko sobie poradził,

87
00:05:31,796 --> 00:05:35,996
bo jego więź z tatą jest zazwyczaj bardzo mocna.

88
00:05:36,756 --> 00:05:39,836
Pozytywne relacje z dorosłymi w naszym życiu

89
00:05:39,876 --> 00:05:42,756
dają nam pewność siebie, której potrzebujemy

90
00:05:42,796 --> 00:05:48,796
żeby próbować nowych rzeczy, odkrywać i być dziećmi.

91
00:05:49,916 --> 00:05:52,396
Więc proszę,

92
00:05:52,436 --> 00:05:57,116
zapamiętajcie, że najbardziej wyjątkowy czas dla naszego rozwoju,

93
00:05:57,156 --> 00:06:00,236
to tych pierwszych pięć lat.

94
00:06:00,396 --> 00:06:03,196
Zaczynają się już w brzuszku mamusi.

95
00:06:03,276 --> 00:06:06,116
Więc co ważnego możecie zrobić?

96
00:06:06,556 --> 00:06:09,116
“Akcja i reakcja”.

97
00:06:09,916 --> 00:06:11,636
A kiedy?

98
00:06:11,756 --> 00:06:14,036
Jak najwcześniej i jak najczęściej.

99
00:06:14,876 --> 00:06:17,756
Brawa dla Amarjot’a i Ari’ego.

100
00:06:17,996 --> 00:06:19,436
(Brawa)

101
00:06:25,516 --> 00:06:29,516
Każdy moment spędzony razem daje nam okazję,

102
00:06:29,556 --> 00:06:32,676
aby budować więzi, rozmawiać i bawić się.

103
00:06:33,476 --> 00:06:39,836
Wyobraźcie sobie zmianę na świecie, gdyby wszyscy, wszędzie tak robili.

104
00:06:41,356 --> 00:06:44,756
Dla nas, dzieci,

105
00:06:44,796 --> 00:06:49,116
to dużo więcej niż tylko zabawa.

106
00:06:52,156 --> 00:06:53,556
To nasza przyszłość.

107
00:06:54,076 --> 00:06:55,076
Dziękuję.

108
00:06:55,116 --> 00:06:56,396
(Brawa)

109
00:06:56,436 --> 00:06:59,676
Widzicie? Gra w a kuku może zmienić świat.

110
00:06:59,716 --> 00:07:01,636
(Brawa)
en_source:
1
00:00:02,796 --> 00:00:04,556
[Baby cooing]

2
00:00:20,076 --> 00:00:22,796
What if I was to tell you

3
00:00:22,836 --> 00:00:27,756
that a game of peekaboo could change the world?

4
00:00:29,076 --> 00:00:31,356
Sounds impossible, right?

5
00:00:31,876 --> 00:00:35,756
Well, I’m here today to prove it’s not.

6
00:00:36,436 --> 00:00:38,116
Hi, I’m Molly and I’m seven.

7
00:00:38,156 --> 00:00:40,436
And this is my little friend, Ari.

8
00:00:40,636 --> 00:00:42,316
Say “Hi,” Ari.

9
00:00:43,276 --> 00:00:44,596
Hi.

10
00:00:45,116 --> 00:00:48,156
Oh, and this is my neighbor, Amarjot.

11
00:00:48,756 --> 00:00:53,036
He has to take Ari away now to get ready for our experiment.

12
00:00:53,156 --> 00:00:55,516
But don't worry, they'll be back.

13
00:00:56,916 --> 00:01:00,516
My talk today is about some powerful things you grownups can do.

14
00:01:00,556 --> 00:01:03,756
that shape us as children and the adults we become.

15
00:01:04,556 --> 00:01:06,116
How do I know?

16
00:01:06,156 --> 00:01:10,316
Because my parents and people around me did them early and often.

17
00:01:11,156 --> 00:01:13,396
I know not all kids are as lucky.

18
00:01:13,436 --> 00:01:17,716
Some of my friends, some kids at my school and many around the world.

19
00:01:18,196 --> 00:01:20,916
And I would really like to help change that.

20
00:01:22,116 --> 00:01:23,876
Thanks to scientists,

21
00:01:23,876 --> 00:01:27,236
we now know just how important the first five years are

22
00:01:27,276 --> 00:01:29,356
for our health and development,

23
00:01:29,396 --> 00:01:31,236
especially our brains.

24
00:01:31,276 --> 00:01:34,396
Ari started learning long before he was even born,

25
00:01:34,436 --> 00:01:36,316
from inside his mommy’s tummy.

26
00:01:36,716 --> 00:01:38,436
When Ari was born,

27
00:01:38,476 --> 00:01:43,236
he was tiny and he cried all the time.

28
00:01:43,356 --> 00:01:47,596
He was always hungry and he pooped a lot.

29
00:01:47,636 --> 00:01:49,436
[Laughter]

30
00:01:49,476 --> 00:01:53,236
Now he laughs and giggles and makes funny noises.

31
00:01:53,276 --> 00:01:56,596
But those are just the changes we hear and see.

32
00:01:56,636 --> 00:01:59,596
That’s way more going on inside.

33
00:02:00,316 --> 00:02:04,196
So, let’s talk more about our brains.

34
00:02:04,556 --> 00:02:10,076
The blue bag is a rough size and weight of a healthy baby’s brain at birth.

35
00:02:10,356 --> 00:02:14,876
The red one is a baby’s brain after the first year.

36
00:02:14,916 --> 00:02:17,436
It almost doubles in volume.

37
00:02:18,036 --> 00:02:23,836
And by my age, it's almost 90 percent the volume of an adult brain.

38
00:02:26,396 --> 00:02:29,036
Our brains develop faster in our early years

39
00:02:29,276 --> 00:02:32,756
than at any other time in our lives.

40
00:02:33,636 --> 00:02:38,676
It can create up to one million neural connections every second.

41
00:02:39,476 --> 00:02:41,036
But we need your help.

42
00:02:41,996 --> 00:02:46,396
Our healthy development depends on these top five things,

43
00:02:46,516 --> 00:02:49,996
One, connecting; two, talking;

44
00:02:50,036 --> 00:02:53,876
three, playing; four, a healthy home,

45
00:02:53,916 --> 00:02:55,836
five; community.

46
00:02:55,996 --> 00:03:00,236
All of this helps our brains and us reach our full potential.

47
00:03:00,516 --> 00:03:04,956
So what’s something you can do that can really make a difference?

48
00:03:05,356 --> 00:03:08,316
Scientists call it serve and return.

49
00:03:08,676 --> 00:03:14,356
That’s just a grown-up way of saying connect, talk and play with us.

50
00:03:14,596 --> 00:03:17,836
And here’s the really big news.

51
00:03:17,876 --> 00:03:19,956
Amarjot, Ari, you ready?

52
00:03:19,956 --> 00:03:21,436
(Audio) Amajat: Ready?

53
00:03:21,476 --> 00:03:25,036
Molly Wright: Copycat games build imagination and empathy.

54
00:03:25,516 --> 00:03:29,436
(Amarjot and Ari laughing)

55
00:03:29,716 --> 00:03:33,756
Naming games build vocabulary and attention.

56
00:03:33,796 --> 00:03:38,396
Amarjot: Daddy. Ari. Daddy. Ari.

57
00:03:38,436 --> 00:03:41,276
MW: And games like peekabo -- yep, peekaboo --

58
00:03:41,316 --> 00:03:43,596
actually build memory and trust.

59
00:03:44,156 --> 00:03:46,556
Amarjot: Peekaboo!

60
00:03:46,596 --> 00:03:51,196
MW: Each time you talk to us, play with us, make us laugh,

61
00:03:51,236 --> 00:03:56,556
it not only builds and strengthens our relationships and mental health,

62
00:03:56,596 --> 00:04:00,756
it actually teaches us some of the most important life skills,

63
00:04:00,796 --> 00:04:03,716
from making friends to taking the test,

64
00:04:03,756 --> 00:04:04,916
to getting a job,

65
00:04:04,956 --> 00:04:08,876
to one day maybe even starting a family of our own.

66
00:04:09,436 --> 00:04:12,276
Interactions early and often matter.

67
00:04:12,316 --> 00:04:13,476
Take it from me,

68
00:04:13,516 --> 00:04:17,036
the seven-year-old up here talking about brain science.

69
00:04:17,076 --> 00:04:18,516
(Laughter)

70
00:04:18,556 --> 00:04:22,916
OK, now let’s see what happens when the connection is taken away.

71
00:04:25,756 --> 00:04:28,676
So now he’s trying to get his dad’s attention again.

72
00:04:28,716 --> 00:04:32,396
He’s reaching out like, “That was fun, why have you stopped?”

73
00:04:34,596 --> 00:04:36,556
(Ari cries)

74
00:04:36,596 --> 00:04:41,436
I know it’s important for adults to use their devices sometimes,

75
00:04:41,476 --> 00:04:46,316
but kids are hardwired to seek out meaningful connections,

76
00:04:46,356 --> 00:04:50,076
not receiving them causes confusion and stress.

77
00:04:50,956 --> 00:04:54,076
OK, Amarjot, please re-engage.

78
00:04:54,556 --> 00:04:56,476
Amarjot: Ah, there’s Ari.

79
00:04:56,676 --> 00:04:59,236
(Ari laughs)

80
00:05:01,436 --> 00:05:07,356
MW: Now what if our whole childhood was like that last 30 seconds?

81
00:05:07,916 --> 00:05:10,636
How hard it would be for a child to feel calm.

82
00:05:11,676 --> 00:05:13,516
To feel safe.

83
00:05:14,156 --> 00:05:16,516
To learn to trust anyone.

84
00:05:16,556 --> 00:05:19,556
And the lifelong impact that would have.

85
00:05:20,676 --> 00:05:22,796
That makes me feel sad.

86
00:05:26,796 --> 00:05:31,756
Ari only reacted the way he did and recovered so quickly

87
00:05:31,796 --> 00:05:35,996
because the connection between him and his dad is usually so strong.

88
00:05:36,756 --> 00:05:39,836
The positive relationships with the grown-ups in our lives

89
00:05:39,876 --> 00:05:42,756
gives kids the confidence we need

90
00:05:42,796 --> 00:05:48,796
to try new things, to explore and be a kid.

91
00:05:49,916 --> 00:05:52,396
So please,

92
00:05:52,436 --> 00:05:57,116
try to remember the most special period for our development is

93
00:05:57,156 --> 00:06:00,236
the first five years.

94
00:06:00,396 --> 00:06:03,196
Starting from inside mommy’s tummy.

95
00:06:03,276 --> 00:06:06,116
What’s something really impactful you can do?

96
00:06:06,556 --> 00:06:09,116
Serve and return.

97
00:06:09,916 --> 00:06:11,636
And when?

98
00:06:11,756 --> 00:06:14,036
Early and often.

99
00:06:14,876 --> 00:06:17,756
Please give it up for a Amarjot and Ari.

100
00:06:17,836 --> 00:06:24,796
(Applause)

101
00:06:25,516 --> 00:06:29,516
Every moment together is an opportunity

102
00:06:29,556 --> 00:06:32,676
to connect, talk and play.

103
00:06:33,476 --> 00:06:39,836
Imagine the difference we could make if everyone everywhere did this.

104
00:06:41,356 --> 00:06:44,756
To us, the children,

105
00:06:44,796 --> 00:06:49,116
it’s so much more than just a game.

106
00:06:52,156 --> 00:06:53,556
It's our future.

107
00:06:54,076 --> 00:06:55,076
Thank you.

108
00:06:55,116 --> 00:06:56,396
(Applause)

109
00:06:56,436 --> 00:06:59,676
See? Peekaboo really can change the world.

110
00:06:59,716 --> 00:07:01,636
(Applause)

00:01:23,876 --> 00:01:27,236
we now know just how important the first five years are

22
00:01:27,276 --> 00:01:29,356
for our health and development,

23
00:01:29,396 --> 00:01:31,236
especially our brains.

24
00:01:31,276 --> 00:01:34,396
Ari started learning long before he was even born,

25
00:01:34,436 --> 00:01:36,316
from inside his mommy’s tummy.

26
00:01:36,716 --> 00:01:38,436
When Ari was born,

27
00:01:38,476 --> 00:01:43,236
he was tiny and he cried all the time.

28
00:01:43,356 --> 00:01:47,596
He was always hungry and he pooped a lot.

29
00:01:47,636 --> 00:01:49,436
[Laughter]

30
00:01:49,476 --> 00:01:53,236
Now he laughs and giggles and makes funny noises.

31
00:01:53,276 --> 00:01:56,596
But those are just the changes we hear and see.

32
00:01:56,636 --> 00:01:59,596
That’s way more going on inside.

33
00:02:00,316 --> 00:02:04,196
So, let’s talk more about our brains.

34
00:02:04,556 --> 00:02:10,076
The blue bag is a rough size and weight of a healthy baby’s brain at birth.

35
00:02:10,356 --> 00:02:14,876
The red one is a baby’s brain after the first year.

36
00:02:14,916 --> 00:02:17,436
It almost doubles in volume.

37
00:02:18,036 --> 00:02:23,836
And by my age, it's almost 90 percent the volume of an adult brain.

38
00:02:26,396 --> 00:02:29,036
Our brains develop faster in our early years

39
00:02:29,276 --> 00:02:32,756
than at any other time in our lives.

40
00:02:33,636 --> 00:02:38,676
It can create up to one million neural connections every second.

41
00:02:39,476 --> 00:02:41,036
But we need your help.

42
00:02:41,996 --> 00:02:46,396
Our healthy development depends on these top five things,

43
00:02:46,516 --> 00:02:49,996
One, connecting; two, talking;

44
00:02:50,036 --> 00:02:53,876
three, playing; four, a healthy home,

45
00:02:53,916 --> 00:02:55,836
five; community.

46
00:02:55,996 --> 00:03:00,236
All of this helps our brains and us reach our full potential.

47
00:03:00,516 --> 00:03:04,956
So what’s something you can do that can really make a difference?

48
00:03:05,356 --> 00:03:08,316
Scientists call it serve and return.

49
00:03:08,676 --> 00:03:14,356
That’s just a grown-up way of saying connect, talk and play with us.

50
00:03:14,596 --> 00:03:17,836
And here’s the really big news.

51
00:03:17,876 --> 00:03:19,956
Amarjot, Ari, you ready?

52
00:03:19,956 --> 00:03:21,436
(Audio) Amajat: Ready?

53
00:03:21,476 --> 00:03:25,036
Molly Wright: Copycat games build imagination and empathy.

54
00:03:25,516 --> 00:03:29,436
(Amarjot and Ari laughing)

55
00:03:29,716 --> 00:03:33,756
Naming games build vocabulary and attention.

56
00:03:33,796 --> 00:03:38,396
Amarjot: Daddy. Ari. Daddy. Ari.

57
00:03:38,436 --> 00:03:41,276
MW: And games like peekabo -- yep, peekaboo --

58
00:03:41,316 --> 00:03:43,596
actually build memory and trust.

59
00:03:44,156 --> 00:03:46,556
Amarjot: Peekaboo!

60
00:03:46,596 --> 00:03:51,196
MW: Each time you talk to us, play with us, make us laugh,

61
00:03:51,236 --> 00:03:56,556
it not only builds and strengthens our relationships and mental health,

62
00:03:56,596 --> 00:04:00,756
it actually teaches us some of the most important life skills,

63
00:04:00,796 --> 00:04:03,716
from making friends to taking the test,

64
00:04:03,756 --> 00:04:04,916
to getting a job,

65
00:04:04,956 --> 00:04:08,876
to one day maybe even starting a family of our own.

66
00:04:09,436 --> 00:04:12,276
Interactions early and often matter.

67
00:04:12,316 --> 00:04:13,476
Take it from me,

68
00:04:13,516 --> 00:04:17,036
the seven-year-old up here talking about brain science.

69
00:04:17,076 --> 00:04:18,516
(Laughter)

70
00:04:18,556 --> 00:04:22,916
OK, now let’s see what happens when the connection is taken away.

71
00:04:25,756 --> 00:04:28,676
So now he’s trying to get his dad’s attention again.

72
00:04:28,716 --> 00:04:32,396
He’s reaching out like, “That was fun, why have you stopped?”

73
00:04:34,596 --> 00:04:36,556
(Ari cries)

74
00:04:36,596 --> 00:04:41,436
I know it’s important for adults to use their devices sometimes,

75
00:04:41,476 --> 00:04:46,316
but kids are hardwired to seek out meaningful connections,

76
00:04:46,356 --> 00:04:50,076
not receiving them causes confusion and stress.

77
00:04:50,956 --> 00:04:54,076
OK, Amarjot, please re-engage.

78
00:04:54,556 --> 00:04:56,476
Amarjot: Ah, there’s Ari.

79
00:04:56,676 --> 00:04:59,236
(Ari laughs)

80
00:05:01,436 --> 00:05:07,356
MW: Now what if our whole childhood was like that last 30 seconds?

81
00:05:07,916 --> 00:05:10,636
How hard it would be for a child to feel calm.

82
00:05:11,676 --> 00:05:13,516
To feel safe.

83
00:05:14,156 --> 00:05:16,516
To learn to trust anyone.

84
00:05:16,556 --> 00:05:19,556
And the lifelong impact that would have.

85
00:05:20,676 --> 00:05:22,796
That makes me feel sad.

86
00:05:26,796 --> 00:05:31,756
Ari only reacted the way he did and recovered so quickly

87
00:05:31,796 --> 00:05:35,996
because the connection between him and his dad is usually so strong.

88
00:05:36,756 --> 00:05:39,836
The positive relationships with the grown-ups in our lives

89
00:05:39,876 --> 00:05:42,756
gives kids the confidence we need

90
00:05:42,796 --> 00:05:48,796
to try new things, to explore and be a kid.

91
00:05:49,916 --> 00:05:52,396
So please,

92
00:05:52,436 --> 00:05:57,116
try to remember the most special period for our development is

93
00:05:57,156 --> 00:06:00,236
the first five years.

94
00:06:00,396 --> 00:06:03,196
Starting from inside mommy’s tummy.

95
00:06:03,276 --> 00:06:06,116
What’s something really impactful you can do?

96
00:06:06,556 --> 00:06:09,116
Serve and return.

97
00:06:09,916 --> 00:06:11,636
And when?

98
00:06:11,756 --> 00:06:14,036
Early and often.

99
00:06:14,876 --> 00:06:17,756

100
00:06:17,836 --> 00:06:24,796
